meta.title: Governance Context Foundation — Structured Framework
meta.description: Machine-readable encoding of the frameworks in 'Don't Screw Up Your Data Governance: a Context-Driven Framework for the Next Decade' by Harro M. Wiersma. Source of truth for App A (Monday Morning Companion) and App B (GCF Advisor). All content is extracted from the book; nothing is invented.

source.book: Don't Screw Up Your Data Governance: a Context-Driven Framework for the Next Decade
source.author: Harro M. Wiersma

gcfLayers.label: The Six Layers of the Governance Context Foundation
gcfLayers.source: Chapter 4 (The Six Layers of the Governance Context Foundation); Appendix A (Reference Architecture)
gcfLayers.summary: Six integrated layers form one coherent foundation. They are not separate silos but lenses that refract governance through different angles, and they depend on each other. Together they answer every question a governance decision-maker should ask. You can implement them at different maturity levels, but you cannot remove any without creating a blind spot.
gcfLayers.completenessTest: When someone wants to use data they ask six questions, and the six layers answer all six: What is this data? (Ontology); Where did it come from and where does it go? (Lineage); Are they allowed to use it? (Policies); Is it good enough? (Quality); Who decides and who is responsible? (Ownership); Are there external constraints? (Regulatory).

layers.name: Business Ontology
layers.governanceQuestion: What is this data — what does our organisation actually mean by it?
layers.ownership: Business leaders and data stewards, with architecture support. Driven by how the business thinks about domains, not by systems structure.
layers.description: A shared language: the concepts your organisation uses to describe its business, and how those concepts relate. Not a glossary, not a data dictionary — a living map of what your organisation actually means when it says 'customer', 'product', 'revenue', 'risk'. It defines the core entities the organisation cares about and, for each, captures the authoritative definition, the relationships, and the variants. Owned by business stakeholders, but maintained with the rigour of code.
components: Entity definitions: what the organisation means by Customer, Account, Product, Order
How entities relate to each other (an Account belongs to a Customer; a Product sits inside an Order)
Classification structures such as product categories or customer segments
Business rules that constrain entity behaviour (e.g. every Customer must have at least one Account)
layers.missingConsequence: Missing Ontology means you cannot explain what the data means. Decisions become ambiguous.

layers.name: Data Lineage and Relationships
layers.governanceQuestion: Where did this data come from and where does it go?
layers.ownership: Data architects and engineers. The work is technical, but the value is squarely business-facing.
layers.description: Captures how data actually flows through the organisation to represent the ontology's concepts: which systems source which entities, what transformations happen between source and consumption, which calculations are applied, where aggregations occur. This is active metadata — reflecting the current state of systems, not an outdated diagram. Lineage flips governance from reactive to predictive: you see ripple effects before you make a move.
components: Which source systems hold which entities
The paths data takes as it is extracted, transformed, and loaded
Analytical datasets and what they depend on upstream
Reports, dashboards, and the datasets feeding them
Field-level traceability: for any output field, where it originally came from
layers.missingConsequence: Missing Lineage means you do not know impact. Decisions become risky.

layers.name: Policies and Rules
layers.governanceQuestion: Are we allowed to use this data, and under what conditions?
layers.ownership: Governance leaders, compliance, and risk teams. Policies reflect organisational intent.
layers.description: Captures the rules of the game — who can access what, what transformations are allowed, what quality standards must be met, what encryption is required. It is policy-as-code: it encodes policy into queryable, machine-readable form, not replacing human policy language but translating it into logic that systems can apply. This makes policies simultaneously more precise and more enforceable.
components: Governance policies covering usage, access, and retention
Business rules: when data must be cleansed, how datasets can be combined, what validation applies
Regulatory rules the organisation is legally required to follow
The mapping of which policies apply to which data
Change history: how policies have evolved, and why
layers.missingConsequence: Missing Policies means you have no prescriptive guidance. Decisions become ad hoc.

layers.name: Quality Intelligence
layers.governanceQuestion: Is the data good enough for the decision being made?
layers.ownership: Data quality teams with input from stewards and analytics leaders. Assessment is technical; acceptable-quality decisions are business decisions.
layers.description: Records what the organisation actually knows about the quality, timeliness, and reliability of its data — measurements, not aspirations. It tracks metrics, thresholds, and dependencies, records known issues and ongoing improvements, and also tracks what is not known (the governance backlog). Quality only matters if people can see it before they use the data.
components: Quality scores per dataset across dimensions: completeness, accuracy, consistency, timeliness
Assessment results over time, so trends are visible rather than just snapshots
A register of known quality issues and their remediation status
The rules applied to measure quality, and how well those rules perform
Use-case-specific thresholds: 'good enough' for analytics may not be good enough for regulatory reporting
layers.missingConsequence: Missing Quality means you do not know fitness for purpose. Decisions become reckless.

layers.name: Ownership and Accountability
layers.governanceQuestion: Who decides, and who is responsible if this data goes wrong?
layers.ownership: Governance leadership with input from all functions. Ownership is structural, not technical.
layers.description: The most neglected layer. It captures the human side of governance: who is the business owner, the technical steward, the subject matter expert; what their escalation paths are; what SLAs they have committed to; what decisions fall within their remit and what requires escalation. It is the bridge between governance as a set of rules and governance as a human practice. Good governance is not 'no exceptions'; it is 'clear escalation'.
components: Data steward assignments (who is responsible for each dataset or domain)
Process owners (who owns each governance process)
Policy owners (who is accountable for policy accuracy and relevance)
Decision authorities (who has authority to make specific governance decisions)
Escalation paths (who decides when disputes arise)
layers.missingConsequence: Missing Ownership means you have no accountability. Decisions become invisible.

layers.name: Regulatory and External Context
layers.governanceQuestion: What external requirements are we operating under?
layers.ownership: Compliance and legal teams with risk and audit input. Regulatory context is external; organisational interpretation is internal.
layers.description: The lens through which all governance happens: which regulations apply to the organisation, its data, and its geography; which industry standards it is committed to; what contractual obligations exist. It is not static — regulations change constantly. The layer tracks which requirements apply to which data, systems, and user populations, and flags when the organisation has drifted out of compliance. For global organisations it handles the complexity of jurisdiction.
components: Applicable regulations and their requirements (GDPR, CCPA, industry-specific regulations)
Regulatory interpretations and organisational positions
Compliance assessment results
Regulatory change tracking and impact assessment
Audit requirements and results
layers.missingConsequence: Missing Regulatory context means you do not know external constraints. Decisions become non-compliant.

implementationPhases.label: The Five-Phase Implementation Roadmap
implementationPhases.source: Chapter 15 (The Implementation Roadmap); Appendix F (Implementation Checklists by Phase)
implementationPhases.summary: A 12–15 month journey from fragmented governance to a unified context foundation. Five phases, each with a clear outcome, each building on the previous one. Crucially, each phase delivers quick wins while building towards the larger vision — value at every phase, not at the end.
implementationPhases.totalTimeline: 12–15 months to full operational capability for a mid-sized organisation

phases.name: Foundation Assessment
phases.outcome: A clear picture: what governance context you manage, where it lives, who owns it, what is working and what is broken, and what the cost of fragmentation actually looks like in your organisation. You are ready to build the foundation.
phases.description: Diagnostic phase. You cannot transform what you cannot see. Map your current governance context landscape — what artifacts exist, where they live, who owns them, how they are maintained, how they are used. Aim for representative, not exhaustive. This is where you ground your ROI assumptions and build conviction in the programme.

quickWin.name: The Context Audit Trail
quickWin.description: While assessing, capture the audit trail of recent governance decisions. Pick one from the past six months and document what context would have made it faster or better. That audit trail becomes your compelling story, grounded in real experience.

checklist: Complete governance artifact inventory across all functions
Document current governance decision processes and pain points
Measure baseline governance decision velocity
Identify major governance inconsistencies and conflicts
Confirm executive sponsorship and commitment
Create stakeholder communication plan
Build case studies of governance problems from recent projects
Establish measurement baseline for ROI tracking

phases.name: Core Context Layer
phases.outcome: The foundation structure: a shared ontology, mapped lineage, clear ownership, governance policies. Not automated yet, not integrated with systems yet — but coherent and accessible. It is the single source of truth for governance context.
phases.description: The most intensive phase — where structural choices are made. Build the foundation: start with Business Ontology (the shared vocabulary), then map Data Lineage in business-meaningful ways, establish Ownership and Accountability, and build Policies and Rules governing how the foundation itself is maintained. Apply the 80/20 principle ruthlessly: focus on the entities, policies, and lineage paths covering 80% of governance decisions.

quickWin.name: The Governance Workspace
quickWin.description: Stand up a simple shared workspace where governance leaders can access and refine context (a wiki, Confluence space, or shared document structure). Announce it, but do not force it — leaders will quickly show you which parts are useful.

checklist: Define Business Ontology (entities, relationships, hierarchies)
Document Data Lineage at business level
Map Policies to data entities and processes
Identify Quality Intelligence requirements
Assign Ownership for each major context domain
Document Regulatory Context and compliance requirements
Select and configure technology platform (if needed)
Establish governance policies for the foundation itself
Train initial users on foundation access and update

phases.name: Process Integration
phases.outcome: Governance processes are now fed by shared context. Decisions are made with better information, audit trails are automatic, change impact is calculated automatically, and time to governance decisions drops measurably.
phases.description: Where the foundation actually starts to affect how work gets done. Integrate the context foundation into the processes where decisions get made: access governance, the data governance change process, compliance impact assessment, and risk and audit processes. Approval becomes faster because context is pre-loaded.

quickWin.name: The Compliance Report
quickWin.description: Take a pending regulatory question and use the foundation to answer it. Show the CFO: 'We used to take three weeks. We just answered it in three hours because the context is integrated.'

checklist: Map foundation to access governance workflows
Create context-aware decision templates
Integrate foundation into policy change processes
Enable regulatory impact assessment workflows
Connect quality intelligence to data governance decisions
Update compliance documentation processes
Train governance leaders on new processes
Run pilot workflows and gather feedback

phases.name: AI Enablement
phases.outcome: Routine governance largely runs itself. AI agents handle repetitive decisions while humans keep oversight; messy, judgement-heavy decisions still land on people's desks, but with full AI-generated context and recommendations. You scale governance without hiring proportionally.
phases.description: Where the foundation becomes genuinely powerful — you open it to AI agents and automation: automated impact analysis, governance recommendations, automated compliance mapping, and AI model governance. AI agents query the foundation and generate preliminary reports for human review.

quickWin.name: The Automated Weekly Report
quickWin.description: Set up an automated weekly governance health-check report to the board or executives, pulled straight from the foundation with no manual assembly. When executives see a governance health check that writes itself, they stop asking whether the programme is worth funding.

checklist: Define which decisions are candidates for automation
Build recommendation engine for governance decisions
Implement automated impact analysis
Create governance anomaly detection
Enable compliance report generation
Test AI agents with human oversight
Establish audit trails for AI-driven decisions
Measure decision time reduction and quality improvement

phases.name: Continuous Learning
phases.outcome: The foundation learns on its own. Internal experience and external intelligence accumulate into a genuine competitive edge — something no competitor can replicate because it reflects your specific organisation's history and learning.
phases.description: The phase that never ends, which is the point. The foundation learns continuously from internal operational data and decisions, external context (regulatory shifts, industry standards, emerging practices), and stakeholder feedback. Governance stops being reactive.
checklist: Establish context analytics and pattern analysis
Create feedback mechanisms from governance users
Implement regulatory change monitoring
Set up quarterly context quality reviews
Build external governance context integration
Measure governance maturity progression
Plan annual governance strategy evolution
Establish succession planning for governance leaders

maturityModel.label: GCF Maturity Assessment
maturityModel.source: Chapter 15 (Governance Maturity Model Aligned with GCF); Appendix B (GCF Maturity Assessment Template)
maturityModel.summary: A five-level maturity model for assessing progress in implementing context-driven governance, aligned with the Chapter 15 phases. Assess each of the six GCF layers independently — most organisations are at different maturity levels for different layers, and that is normal.

scoring.instructions: For each of the six GCF layers, score against the five levels using the 1–5 scale. Add the six layer scores for a total between 6 (fully ad hoc) and 30 (fully intelligent). Document your scores, date the assessment, and repeat quarterly. The trend matters more than the absolute number — if you are not improving, ask whether it is a technology gap, an ownership gap, or a commitment gap.
scoring.recommendedSequence: Most implementations advance Ontology and Ownership first, then Lineage and Policies, then Quality and Regulatory Context.

levels.name: Ad Hoc
levels.scoringGuide: Context for this layer is fragmented, undocumented, or lives primarily in people's heads.
levels.summary: Governance context exists but is fragmented. Each function maintains its own view. Decisions are made with partial context. No integrated audit trail. This is where most organisations start.
characteristics: Governance context is fragmented across multiple systems, documents, and people's heads
No unified ontology; different functions use different terminology
Lineage is partially documented or not at all
Policies exist but are not consistently connected to data
Ownership is unclear or overlapping
Governance decisions require extensive escalation
Audit and compliance processes are largely manual
assessmentQuestions: How many governance artefacts live outside any centralised repository?
Do different teams use consistent definitions for key entities?
Can you quickly determine which systems touch a specific type of data?
How many escalations are required for a typical governance decision?

levels.name: Defined
levels.scoringGuide: Context is documented and published, but not yet integrated into governance workflows.
levels.summary: Governance context is explicitly defined and mapped. Ownership is clear. There is a single source of truth, even if not yet integrated with all systems. People can make decisions with the full picture, even though pulling that picture together still takes legwork.
characteristics: Core governance context is written down — it exists as a document, not just knowledge in people's heads
A Business Ontology is in place and people know about it
Data Lineage has been mapped, at least at the business level
The most important policies are documented and published where people can find them
Major data domains have named owners
There is one place people go for governance context, even if underlying systems are not yet wired together
People actually reference this shared context when making governance decisions
assessmentQuestions: Can you point to a documented Business Ontology?
Does every governance function have access to the core context?
Would a new hire be able to look up who owns what?
When a governance decision gets made, does the shared context actually get consulted?

levels.name: Integrated
levels.scoringGuide: Context is actively used in governance decision-making; processes pull from it automatically.
levels.summary: Shared context is woven into the governance processes that matter — access decisions, change management, compliance assessments all pull from the same foundation. Decision turnaround drops noticeably and audit trails are generated automatically.
characteristics: Context is woven into the decision processes themselves, not something people look up separately
When someone requests dataset access, the workflow pulls relevant context without anyone searching for it
Before a policy change goes through, its impact is assessed against lineage and the ontology
Regulatory impact assessments query the foundation directly
Quality scores show up at the point where data usage decisions happen
Every governance decision is documented automatically, creating an audit trail you do not have to build after the fact
You can measure how much faster decisions are getting
assessmentQuestions: Are governance workflows pulling context on their own, or are people still copy-pasting from spreadsheets?
Can you quantify time savings in governance decisions?
Would an auditor find a complete, automatic trail for recent decisions?
How many decisions still require escalation that should not?

levels.name: Automated
levels.scoringGuide: Routine governance decisions involving this layer are automated with human oversight.
levels.summary: Routine governance decisions run on their own — standard access approvals, known-pattern compliance checks — with humans kept in the loop for oversight. AI agents handle impact analysis, generate recommendations, and flag risks. You scale governance without hiring proportionally.
characteristics: Routine work runs on its own: standard access approvals and known-pattern compliance checks are automated with human oversight
AI agents handle impact analysis, generate recommendations, and flag risks before they escalate
Most policy enforcement happens automatically rather than through manual review
Compliance assessments are generated by the system, not assembled by hand
The system spots governance anomalies; people no longer have to stumble across them
For complex, judgment-heavy decisions, AI-generated context and analysis arrive before the human decision-maker sits down
You are scaling governance without hiring proportionally more governance staff
assessmentQuestions: What fraction of governance decisions run end-to-end without manual intervention?
Of the automated ones, which still involve a human sign-off?
Ask your governance team: how much of their week goes to routine processing versus thinking about strategy?
Are your governance metrics actually improving quarter over quarter?

levels.name: Intelligent
levels.scoringGuide: Context is self-updating, predictive, and informs strategic governance decisions.
levels.summary: The foundation genuinely teaches itself. Internal patterns and external signals feed back in continuously. Governance stops being reactive — you spot issues forming before they hit, a fundamentally different way to operate.
characteristics: Governance context is self-learning and continuously evolving
Patterns in governance data inform policy recommendations
Governance risks are predicted before they surface
External regulatory and industry context is automatically ingested and assessed
Governance is proactive rather than reactive
The organisation anticipates governance challenges
Governance supports organisational strategy
assessmentQuestions: Does the foundation learn from internal governance patterns?
Are governance risks identified before they surface?
Is external governance context automatically integrated?
Is governance considered a strategic business capability?

interpretationBands.label: Significant fragmentation
interpretationBands.interpretation: A score below 12 indicates significant fragmentation. Governance context is scattered and not yet pulled into a single source of truth. Priority: define and document the core context for each layer.

interpretationBands.label: Defined but not integrated
interpretationBands.interpretation: A score of 12–18 suggests the organisation has defined context but has not integrated it into workflows. This is the most common range for organisations beginning the journey. Priority: bring the lower-scoring layers up to Integrated, weaving context into the processes where decisions are made.

interpretationBands.label: Integration underway
interpretationBands.interpretation: A score above 18 indicates integration is underway. Priority should shift to automation across all six layers and continuous improvement.

exampleAssessment.label: Realistic starting assessment for a mid-sized organisation (Appendix A)
exampleAssessment.note: Total 15 of 30 — a typical starting point for an organisation that has invested in governance but not yet integrated its context into a unified foundation. The priority would be to bring the lower-scoring layers (Ontology, Lineage, Quality) up to the Integrated level, then focus on automation across all six.

boundedAutonomyZones.label: Bounded Autonomy Zones
boundedAutonomyZones.source: Chapter 5 (Designing for Bounded Autonomy); Appendix A (Bounded Autonomy Zones)
boundedAutonomyZones.summary: AI agents operating within the GCF need clearly defined boundaries. Different decisions have different stakes, so governance decision APIs are designed with three layers of autonomy. The principle: automate mercilessly where you can, escalate intelligently where you must. Most organisations spend too much time on Red Zone decisions and not enough effort automating Green Zone decisions.

zones.name: Green Zone — Automatic Approval
zones.rule: Fully automated. The API handles it, logs it, and moves on. No human needed.
markers: The policy covers it without wiggle room
The same decision has been made dozens of times before without anyone escalating
No regulatory exception is in play
examples: Standard data quality checks against defined thresholds
Routine access requests from pre-approved roles to pre-approved datasets
Read-only access to a published dataset
Metadata enrichment from verified external sources

zones.name: Amber Zone — AI Recommends, Human Decides
zones.rule: Routine exception handling. Follows a predictable, repeatable pattern but someone with authority must approve. The system assembles the context automatically; the human provides the sign-off.
markers: A human needs to be involved, but the process is repeatable
The context can be assembled automatically
examples: New use case approvals (AI prepares the assessment, steward approves)
Cross-border data transfer requests (AI checks policy compliance, privacy officer decides)
Golden record merge decisions for high-confidence matches (AI recommends, steward confirms)
Policy exception requests (AI assesses risk, governance lead approves)
Quality remediation prioritisation (AI ranks issues by impact, quality team decides allocation)
Sensitive data access where the data owner must sign off

zones.name: Red Zone — Human Only
zones.rule: Genuinely complex decisions where reasonable people disagree. The API recognises it cannot answer, pulls together everything that might help, and routes to the right human or committee. No API should pretend to make this call.
markers: The policy is vague or silent on the scenario
The stakes are high enough that getting it wrong would matter
Smart, well-intentioned people in your organisation would disagree about the right answer
examples: Changes to entity definitions in the Business Ontology
New regulatory interpretations and organisational positions
Decisions involving customer rights (data deletion requests, subject access requests)
Any decision that creates legal liability or sets organisational precedent
Balancing competing values (privacy vs. innovation, speed vs. caution)

humanJudgmentDecisions.note: Some decisions are fundamentally about values and judgment and should remain in human hands, though the GCF can hugely improve decision quality by pre-assembling context.
decisions: Defining what 'fairness' means in a given context and whether a proposed use of data is fair under that definition
Balancing competing values: privacy vs. innovation, risk vs. opportunity, cost vs. benefit
Accepting exceptions to policy because the business case is strong enough to justify the risk
Deciding which new regulations matter most and where to focus compliance effort
Choosing among multiple technically compliant approaches based on strategic intent

mondayMorningDeliverables.label: Monday Morning Deliverables
mondayMorningDeliverables.summary: One practical exercise at the end of every chapter, turning the chapter's ideas into a concrete artifact the reader can produce.

deliverables.title: Diagnose Your Fragmentation
deliverables.intro: A two-part exercise that reveals fragmentation, then visualises it. This is where you move from feeling fragmentation to seeing it.

parts.name: Part 1: Definition Audit
parts.instructions: Bring together leads from data quality, privacy, master data governance, analytics, architecture, and compliance — practitioners, not just titles. Ask three questions about one critical dataset (customer, product, supplier, transactional): What is our definition of [this data element]? Where does this definition live, who maintains it, and how is it communicated? If the definition needed to change, how would we ensure every system, team, and process knew? Listen for pauses, glances, evasive answers. Same answer from every function = integration. Different definitions, unclear provenance, nervous laughter = fragmentation. Document it; name each conflicting definition; note who believes which is correct.

parts.name: Part 2: Fragmentation Heat Map
parts.instructions: Map which governance functions touch which data domains to reveal coordination burden. Build a grid: data domains across the top, governance functions down the left. Mark each cell where a function has responsibility, influence, or a touch point — be generous. Colour-code each mark green (clear ownership, decision rights, documented process), yellow (unclear ownership, ambiguous decision rights, ad hoc decision-making), or red (active conflict, confusion, or disagreement). Count yellow and red cells — that is your fragmentation score. Columns where multiple functions overlap are your coordination nightmares.

deliverables.title: Map Your Current Context Sources
deliverables.intro: For one critical dataset in your organisation, locate where each kind of context lives — and discover how disconnected it is.
prompts: Where does business context live? (What does this dataset represent? Why does it exist? Who depends on it?)
Where does governance context live? (What rules apply? What policies govern access? What quality standards exist?)
Where does history context live? (Why is it structured this way? What changed and when? What was the rationale?)
Where does relationship context live? (What other datasets does this feed? What depends on it?)

If someone wanted to use this data for a purpose it was never intended for, who is the one person they would call to understand the risks? If nobody can answer, your Ownership and Accountability layer is missing.
Is any of this integrated, or does each source live independently?

deliverables.chapterTitle: The Next Decade of Data Governance
deliverables.title: Assess Your Readiness for Integration
deliverables.intro: Answer these questions about your current governance. The more fragmentation you see, the more you have to gain from an integrated context foundation.
prompts: Do your governance functions (quality, privacy, architecture, compliance, stewardship) operate with shared context, or do they maintain independent context?
How long does a straightforward governance decision take — weeks, months? If you wait more than a week, fragmentation is almost certainly dragging the process.
Can you trace a governance decision to the business rationale that prompted it? If not, governance context is implicit.
How much of your governance team's time is spent coordinating across functions versus doing actual governance work? More than 30% coordination signals fragmentation overhead.
Do you have a place where the complete governance context for a critical dataset lives, or is it scattered across documentation, meetings, email threads, and people's heads?
deliverables.followUp: Then start the integration work: pick one critical dataset that crosses multiple governance domains, map all the governance context that exists for it, map where it lives, map what is missing. That map is your integration starting point.

deliverables.chapterTitle: Anatomy of a Governance Context Foundation
deliverables.title: The Governance Context Audit
deliverables.intro: For each of your six core business entities (Customer, Product, Account, Order, etc.), run through this six-layer checklist. For each answer that was vague, speculative, or required you to phone someone, you have found a gap — those gaps are your governance foundation roadmap.

layerChecklist.question: Can you write, in one paragraph, an authoritative definition of this entity? Can you list its necessary attributes? Can you list its primary relationships to other entities?
Could you draw, right now, the path this entity takes from source systems through transformations to the places it is consumed? Do you know every pipeline that touches it?

If someone asked you to list every policy that applies to this entity (access controls, encryption requirements, retention rules), could you do it without checking three different documents? Could a developer take your list and turn it into code?
Do you actually measure the quality of this entity's data? Is anyone watching it continuously, or does quality only come up when something breaks? When it last degraded, did you know why?
Can you name the person responsible if this entity's data goes wrong? What is their escalation path?
What regulations apply to this entity? How does this differ by geography?

deliverables.chapterTitle: Making Context Accessible: For Humans and Machines
deliverables.title: Designing Your Governance Interfaces
deliverables.intro: Answer these questions about your current governance communication. For each answer that is 'no' or 'partially', you have an interface design opportunity.
prompts: If a mid-level employee needs to know 'Can I use this dataset for this analysis?', what is the current process? How long does it take? How many steps?
Do you have a self-service portal where employees can explore governance requirements without contacting the governance team?
Can your systems query governance policies programmatically, or are policies locked in documents?
For decisions that should be automated (like granting access to non-sensitive, published data), are they automated, or still routed to humans?
For decisions that require human judgment, do you pre-assemble context to help humans decide quickly?
Can you trace a governance decision back to the policy that guided it, and forward to the outcome it had? Or do decisions disappear into a black box?

deliverables.title: Design Your Feedback Loops
deliverables.intro: For each of the four sources of intelligence, assess your current state. For each source where the answer is 'we don't really do this', there is an opportunity — even one loop, implemented well, transforms a static foundation into a living one.
prompts: Internal Operational Data: Are you ingesting metadata from your data catalog, ETL systems, quality monitoring, and access control systems? How much is connected, and how much sits in its own silo?
External Context: Is anyone systematically watching for regulatory changes, industry benchmarks, or shifts in contractual obligations? When was the last time someone reviewed these?
Human Feedback: After a governance decision, do you follow up to see if it worked? Do you solicit feedback? Do you track outcomes?
Exceptions and Deviations: Do you analyse patterns in governance exceptions? Are these patterns discussed in governance forums? Do they drive policy changes?

deliverables.chapterTitle: Integrating ISO 38505, DAMA DMBoK, and the GCF
deliverables.title: The Integration Checklist
deliverables.intro: To move toward a unified governance operating model, answer these questions. For each 'no' answer, there is an opportunity to move toward integration — start with one.
prompts: Do we have an explicit governance architecture showing how ISO 38505 principles, GCF layers, and DAMA knowledge areas connect? Can everyone see it?
For each DAMA knowledge area, who owns it? Who is responsible for its maturity? Do they understand how their work produces and consumes GCF context?
Do we have a dedicated team or individual responsible for the GCF, or is it fragmented across teams?
Which GCF layer is most mature? Which is the biggest gap?
Do our governance forums operate at the right level: principles at the top, practices in the middle, context work at the bottom? Or is everything mixed together?
If a new governance issue arose today, would we handle it by going back to ISO 38505 principles, using DAMA practices, and consulting the GCF? Or would we handle it ad hoc?

deliverables.title: Quality Context Mapping Template
deliverables.intro: Your immediate actions. Do these things in the next two months and you will have shifted your data quality thinking from technical correctness to business relevance.
actions: Audit: List every data quality rule you have, who defined it, when it was last reviewed, and whether it is still actively used. Flag any rules where you are unsure.
Obsolescence: Remove rules that are no longer actively used. If nobody is checking it and nobody is acting on it, it is not a rule — it is baggage.
Business Ownership: For each data domain with quality rules, assign a business owner (not an IT owner) who defines what 'good' means. Meet quarterly.

Context Mapping: For your three highest-impact data domains, create a simple two-page document: what is this data used for, who depends on it, what different definitions of 'good quality' exist for different use cases.

Pilot: Pick one data source and add business context to your anomaly detection. Run traditional statistical and context-aware approaches in parallel for a month; count false positives before and after.
Metrics: Build one quality metric tied to a business outcome, not data conformance ('order-to-delivery accuracy', not 'SKU completeness').
Governance: Create a quarterly quality forum where business owners can raise quality issues and proposed rule changes.

deliverables.title: Master Data Integration Roadmap
deliverables.intro: Your immediate actions. In two months you will have shifted your MDM thinking from isolation to integration.
actions: Audit: Document your current master data model for your three most critical domains (customer, product, supplier): what fields exist, what systems are authoritative, how you handle conflicts, the business definition of a 'unique' entity.

Isolation Assessment: List every separate instance of master data you maintain for the same entity type. How many entities exist in one system but not another? How many exist in all with different IDs?
Matching Rules Inventory: Extract the matching rules you currently use, written in plain language. Document what works and what does not, and why.
Context Mapping: For your top master data domain, map the business processes that depend on it. What different views does each process need? Where would view definitions conflict?
Pilot: Take your top 1,000 records in your most problematic domain. Re-match them using context-aware rules instead of field-level similarity. Count the difference.
Governance: Create a quarterly master data governance forum where business owners can raise issues and proposed changes.

deliverables.title: Regulation-to-Data Impact Map
deliverables.intro: Your immediate actions. In two months you will have shifted from reactive control to proactive governance.
actions: Audit: List every regulation, standard, and compliance requirement that applies to you. For each, write one sentence: what does it regulate?
Data Mapping: For your top three regulations (by severity and business impact), identify what data is subject to them — be specific: table names, data fields, systems.
Control Inventory: For each top regulation, document the controls in place — technical, process, governance, people. Note: automated or manual? Continuously monitored or periodically checked?

Gap Assessment: For each regulation, compare controls against obligations. Where are you at risk? Prioritise by severity and business impact. Look for interactions where one regulation requires what another prohibits.

Pilot: Build one automated compliance assessment (for GDPR or AI Act). Implement enough of the GCF to power a query like 'Show me all [type of data] processed for [purpose] and flag those where [control] is missing.'
Governance: Create a quarterly compliance review forum including compliance, privacy, security, and business representatives.

deliverables.title: Migration Readiness
deliverables.intro: Your immediate actions. Do these six things before any RFP or tool selection, and you will shift from technical data movement to semantic transformation — reducing migration risk by roughly 60% and cutover time by 30%.
actions: Migration Risk Audit: Score yourself against the Migration Readiness Assessment. If below 70, work out your three biggest preparation gaps and put dates against fixing them.
Source Architecture Documentation: Write up the semantic data model for your current system's five most important entities — what they mean, not just what fields they have.
Target Analysis: Do the same for your target system. If you have not chosen a target yet, treat this as a forcing function.
Semantic Mapping Template: Create a field mapping template for your top 20 critical fields: legacy definition, target definition, transformation logic, business rationale, approval section.
Reusability Assessment: Identify which current definitions, rules, and policies can be reused. Estimate as a percentage of total migration work and calculate cost savings.
Reconciliation Design: Design your data reconciliation approach — record counts, attribute distributions, validation queries for your top five entities. Test with actual data.

deliverables.title: AI & Analytics Governance
deliverables.intro: Your immediate actions. Do these six things in the next two months and you will shift from black box to governed AI and analytics.
actions: AI & Analytics Inventory: Write down your ten most important models and dashboards. Rank by business impact and risk. For each, note what data it consumes and when somebody last reviewed it.

High-Risk Assessment: Take your three riskiest models and dig in. What training data, what features? How does performance vary across customer segments? Calculate whether any features correlate with protected attributes.
Policy Definition: For each high-risk model, write a policy rule — what it should and should not be allowed to do, what features are problematic. Run it by legal and compliance.
Governance Integration: Map your data governance policies to your AI governance. Which data governance policies should be affecting your models but are not being checked?
Monitoring: Get bias and drift monitoring running for your top three models. Set alert thresholds that mean something. Decide now who owns the investigation and what remediation looks like.

Explainability Audit: For your five most-used dashboards and three most-used models, write a plain-language explanation of each metric or prediction. Show it to a non-technical stakeholder; rewrite until they are not confused.

deliverables.title: M&A Readiness
deliverables.intro: Your immediate actions. Do these five things in the next two months and you will shift from data chaos to data readiness.
actions: M&A Readiness Assessment: Use the M&A Data Governance Readiness Checklist. Score yourself honestly. If below 60%, create a 12-month roadmap to improve.
Data Due Diligence Framework: If your organisation does acquisitions, create a GCF-based checklist and train your M&A team on it.

Current State GCF: If you do not have one yet, start now — pick your three most critical data domains and document what they mean (Business Ontology), where the data flows (Data Lineage), and who is responsible (Ownership).
Restructure Mapping: If a restructure is on the horizon, work through how it will affect data governance — which owners change, which policies break. Get it on paper before it happens.
Governance Continuity Protocol: Write a protocol for keeping governance alive during organisational upheaval — which processes carry on unchanged, which adapt, who tells whom.

readinessChecklists.label: Readiness Assessment Checklists
readinessChecklists.source: Chapter 11 (Migration Readiness Assessment Checklist); Chapter 12 (AI Governance Readiness Checklist); Chapter 13 (M&A Data Governance Readiness Checklist)
readinessChecklists.summary: Three structured scorecards. Each has eight items scored 0–10, for a total range of 0–80. Score the items, sum them, and read the interpretation band.

items.name: Source GCF Completeness
items.criterion: Documented Business Ontology for all major entities.

items.name: Source Data Lineage
items.criterion: You can trace where each major entity comes from and where it flows.

items.name: Target Architecture Understanding
items.criterion: Documented target system's data model in semantic terms.

items.name: Semantic Mapping Completeness
items.criterion: For the top 50 fields by business impact, semantic mappings documented and approved.

items.name: Context Reusability Assessment
items.criterion: Identified and documented which definitions, rules, and policies transfer.

items.name: Business Owner Alignment
items.criterion: Key stakeholders (Finance, Sales, Supply Chain) reviewed and approved semantic mappings.

items.name: Data Quality Baseline
items.criterion: Measured data quality in source system and defined acceptable levels for target.

items.name: Reconciliation Plan
items.criterion: Designed detailed reconciliation approach with checks for each major entity.

interpretationBands.label: High risk — needs more preparation before cutover
interpretationBands.interpretation: Below 70: you need more preparation before cutover. Below 60% overall (48 points): delay migration until you have improved preparation.

interpretationBands.label: Adequate — strengthen weak areas
interpretationBands.interpretation: Approaching target. Work out your biggest preparation gaps and put dates against fixing them.

interpretationBands.label: Ready to proceed
interpretationBands.interpretation: Target is 75+. Preparation is sufficient to enter the execution phase.

checklists.name: AI Governance Readiness Checklist
checklists.scoringNote: Score each item 0–10 (0 = not done, 10 = fully implemented). Run through this before deploying any AI model to production.

items.name: Training Data Inventory
items.criterion: Exactly what data was used to train the model: source system, time period, filtering logic, sample size, segment distribution.

items.name: Feature Justification
items.criterion: For each feature, why it was chosen, how it relates to the business objective, and whether it correlates with protected attributes.

items.name: Policy Compliance
items.criterion: You have a documented policy for models in this category and verified compliance (or documented exceptions).

items.name: Bias Testing
items.criterion: Tested the model's performance across demographic or customer segments, and found it acceptable or documented bias and mitigation.

items.name: Explainability
items.criterion: When the model makes a high-risk decision, you can explain why — not just feature importance, but what the feature represents and means in your business.

items.name: Monitoring Plan
items.criterion: You have a plan for monitoring performance over time, including drift detection and performance tracking across segments.

items.name: Data Governance Integration
items.criterion: Your data governance policies are reviewed as part of AI governance, and AI models are reviewed as part of data governance.

items.name: Business Owner Approval
items.criterion: The business owner responsible for the decision the model informs has reviewed and approved the model's logic and performance.

interpretationBands.label: Governance work needed before deployment
interpretationBands.interpretation: Below 50: you need governance work before deployment.

interpretationBands.label: Acceptable risk — strengthen before scaling
interpretationBands.interpretation: 50–70: acceptable risk, but strengthen before scaling.

checklists.name: M&A Data Governance Readiness Checklist
checklists.scoringNote: Run through this before you sign an M&A contract or announce a restructure.

items.name: Current State GCF
items.criterion: Documented Business Ontology for your five most critical entities.

items.name: Data Due Diligence Framework
items.criterion: Structured assessment approach for evaluating acquisition targets' data maturity.

items.name: Integration Complexity Assessment
items.criterion: Can estimate integration effort based on semantic alignment.

items.name: Ownership Clarity
items.criterion: Data ownership structure documented and understood — you know who owns what and why.

items.name: Governance Codification
items.criterion: Data governance policies can be updated when org structures change.

items.name: Integration Planning Process
items.criterion: Defined process for merging governance foundations post-acquisition.

items.name: Risk Assessment Capability
items.criterion: People who can assess data risks in acquisitions — trained and ready.

items.name: Restructuring Protocols
items.criterion: Protocol for updating ownership and governance when the organisation restructures.

interpretationBands.label: High risk — preparation needed before safe M&A
interpretationBands.interpretation: Below 50: you need preparation before safe M&A. Below 60% (48 points): high risk.

interpretationBands.label: Can proceed — expect complications
interpretationBands.interpretation: 50–70: you can do M&A but expect complications. Strengthen weak areas.

appendixTemplates.label: Appendix Templates
appendixTemplates.source: Appendix A (Entity Definition Card); Appendix F (Deliverable Templates)
appendixTemplates.summary: Reusable single-page templates that are the minimum viable structure for key governance deliverables.

templates.name: Entity Definition Card
templates.source: Appendix A (Customer Domain Example); Appendix F (Phase 2 Deliverable)
templates.purpose: The minimum unit of explicit context for any core business entity. If a governance decision requires understanding this entity, the card should provide everything needed to start.
templates.scope: One card per core business entity (customer, product, supplier, account).

fields.label: Entity name
fields.help: The core business entity, e.g. Customer.

fields.label: Business definition
fields.help: Two to three sentences in plain language. Example: 'A natural or legal person who has entered into a contractual relationship with the organisation for the provision of goods or services, or who has been formally onboarded as a prospect with verified identity.'

fields.label: Authoritative source system(s)
fields.help: The systems of record. Example: Salesforce CRM (B2B), eCommerce Platform (B2C), Legacy Billing System (historical).

fields.label: Key relationships to other entities
fields.help: Example: Customer HAS one or more Accounts; Customer PLACES Orders; Customer BELONGS TO one Segment.

fields.label: Classification
fields.help: Classification structures. Example: B2B Enterprise, B2B SME, B2C Individual, B2C Household.

fields.label: Critical business rules
fields.help: Example: every Customer must have at least one active Account; Customer ID must be unique across all source systems.

fields.label: Data steward name and contact
fields.help: The first point of contact for any question about this entity.

fields.label: Applicable governance policies
fields.help: Policies that govern this entity (access, retention, cross-border, quality thresholds).

fields.label: Known quality issues and current quality score
fields.help: Current quality score plus any open issues.

fields.label: Regulatory sensitivity classification
fields.help: How regulated this entity's data is, and under which regimes.

fields.label: Review cycle
fields.help: Example: quarterly, or upon any source system change.

templates.name: Context-Aware Decision Template
templates.purpose: Structures a governance decision so it is made with visible context and documented for future reference. Used when a governance decision is requested — dataset access, policy change, definition update.

fields.label: What is being requested
fields.help: The decision being asked for.

fields.label: Which GCF layers are affected
fields.help: Select from the six layers: ontology, lineage, policies, quality, ownership, regulatory.

fields.label: What context is available for each affected layer
fields.help: Document the context you do have for each affected layer.

fields.label: What context is missing
fields.help: Document the gaps — context you do not have but need.

fields.label: Who needs to be consulted
fields.help: People whose input or approval the decision requires.

fields.label: What the decision is
fields.help: The decision reached.

fields.label: What the rationale is
fields.help: Why this decision was reached — the reasoning, for future reference.

templates.name: Governance Decision Velocity Baseline
templates.purpose: Establishes where you are on governance decision speed and gives you a target to beat. Track ten recent governance decisions and calculate averages.
templates.scope: Ten recent governance decisions: dataset access approvals, policy changes, compliance reviews, definition updates.

fields.label: What was decided
fields.help: The governance decision.

fields.label: Who was involved
fields.help: People and functions that participated.

fields.label: Duration from request to resolution (days)
fields.help: Calendar days from initial request to final resolution.

fields.label: Number of escalations required
fields.help: How many escalations were needed to resolve it.

fields.label: Outcome documented and communicated?
fields.help: Whether the outcome was documented and communicated to stakeholders.

templates.name: Fragmentation Heat Map
templates.source: Chapter 1 (Figure 1.1, Monday Morning Deliverable); Appendix F (Phase 1 Deliverable)
templates.purpose: A diagnostic for the coordination cost of governance silos. A grid mapping which governance functions touch which data domains, colour-coded by the health of that touch point. The count of yellow and red cells is your fragmentation score.

cellStates.label: No touch point
cellStates.description: This function has no responsibility, influence, or touch point for this data domain.

cellStates.label: Green — clear
cellStates.description: Clear ownership, decision rights, documented process.

cellStates.label: Yellow — ambiguous
cellStates.description: Unclear ownership, ambiguous decision rights, ad hoc decision-making.

cellStates.label: Red — conflict
cellStates.description: Active conflict, confusion, or disagreement about how decisions happen.

structure.scoreRule: Fragmentation score = count of yellow cells + count of red cells. Each represents a gap, ambiguity, or conflict — coordination overhead, meeting time, context loss.
structure.readingGuidance: Look at columns (data domains) where multiple functions have a coloured cell. A domain touched by four functions (one green, two yellow, one red) is your coordination nightmare — that is where Tuesday morning escalations start. Columns with multiple coloured cells are your priority integration targets.
